elements.sent-2411-001: We initiate the study of multipacking problems for geometric point sets with respect to their Euclidean distances.
elements.sent-2411-002: We consider a set of $n$ points $P$ and define $N_s[v]$ as the subset of $P$ that includes the $s$ nearest points of $v \in P$ and the point $v$ itself.
elements.sent-2411-003: We assume that the $s$-th neighbor of each point is unique, for every $s \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots , n-1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-004: For a natural number $r \leq n$, an $r$-multipacking is a set $ M \subseteq P $ such that for each point $ v \in P $ and for every integer $ 1\leq s \leq r $, $|N_s[v]\cap M|\leq (s+1)/2$.
elements.sent-2411-005: The $r$-multipacking number of $ P $ is the maximum cardinality of an $r$-multipacking of $ P $ and is denoted by $ \MP_{r (P) $.}
elements.sent-2411-006: For $r=n-1$, an $r$-multipacking is called a multipacking and $r$-multipacking number is called as multipacking number.
elements.sent-2411-007: We study the problem of computing a maximum $r$-multipacking for point sets in $\mathbb{R ^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: We show that a maximum $1$-multipacking can be computed in polynomial time but computing a maximum $2$-multipacking is NP complete.
elements.sent-2411-009: Further, we provide approximation and parameterized solutions to the $2$-multipacking problem.
elements.sent-2411-010: The problem of locating facilities, both desirable and undesirable, is of utmost importance due to its huge number of applications in the real world~\cite{wolf2011facility .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Allocation of restricted resources within an arena has been extensively studied across various models and methodologies since the 1980s.
elements.sent-2411-012: Drezner and Wesolowsky did a "maximin" problem where a certain facility must serve a given set of users but must be placed in such a manner that its minimum distance from the users is maximized (see~\cite{drezner1980maximin,drezner1983location ).}
elements.sent-2411-013: Rodriguez et al.~\cite{rodriguez2006general gave a generic model for the obnoxious facility location problem inside a given polygonal region.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Rakas et al. \cite{rakas2004multi devised a multi-objective model to determine the optimal locations for undesirable facilities.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The book by Daskin \cite{daskin1997network contains a nice collection of problems, models, and algorithms regarding obnoxious facility location problems in the domain of supply chain networks.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this paper, we initiate a novel study of the Multipacking \cite{teshima2012broadcasts problem in Euclidean space, which resembles the obnoxious facility location problem in computational geometry.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Given a planar point set $P \subseteq \mathbb{R ^2$ of size $n$, the neighborhood of size $r$ of a point $v \in P$ is the subset of $P$ that consists of the $r$ nearest points of $v$ and $v$ itself.}
elements.sent-2411-019: A natural and realistic assumption here is the distances between all pairs of points are distinct.
elements.sent-2411-020: Thus the $r$-th neighbor of every point is unique.
elements.sent-2411-021: This assumption is easy to achieve through some form of controlled perturbation (see \cite{mehlhorn2006reliable ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: For a natural number $r \leq n$, an $r$-multipacking is a subset $ M $ of $ P $, such that for each point $ v \in P $ and for every integer $ 1\leq s \leq r $, $|N_s[v]\cap M|\leq (s+1)/2$, i.e. $M$ contains at most half of the number of elements of $N_s[v]$.
elements.sent-2411-023: The $r$-multipacking number of $ P $ is the maximum cardinality of an $r$-multipacking of $ P $ and is denoted by $ \MP_r(P) $.
elements.sent-2411-024: An $(n-1)$-multipacking $M$ of $P$ is referred to as multipacking of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-025: The multipacking number of $P$, denoted by $\MP(P)$, refers to the cardinality of a maximum multipacking of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Computing maximum multipacking involves selecting the maximum number of points from the given point set satisfying the constraints of multipacking.
elements.sent-2411-027: This variant of facility location problem is interesting due to their wide application in operation research~\cite{rodriguez2006general , resource allocation~\cite{melachrinoudis1999bicriteria , VLSI design \cite{abravaya2010maximizing , network planning~\cite{cappanera2003discrete,rakas2004multi , and clustering \cite{henzinger2020dynamic .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: To the best of our knowledge, no significant work on maximum multipacking has been done on planar point sets concerning their Euclidean distances.
elements.sent-2411-029: Whereas the literature contains numerous intriguing findings for this problem in graph theory.
elements.sent-2411-030: Multipacking was first discussed by Teshima~\cite{teshima2012broadcasts .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Other related works can be found in~\cite{beaudou2019multipacking,cornuejols2001combinatorial,das2023relation,meir1975relations .}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, as of now, no polynomial-time algorithm is known for finding a maximum multipacking of general graphs, the problem is also not known to be NP-hard.
elements.sent-2411-033: Nonetheless, polynomial-time algorithms exist for trees and strongly chordal graphs~\cite{brewster2019broadcast .}
elements.sent-2411-034: For further references on algorithmic results regarding multipacking problem on graphs, we refer to the paper~\cite{foucaud2021complexity .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Further, we define the function $\MMP_{r (t)$ to be the smallest number such that any point set $P \subset \mathbb{R ^2$ of size $ \MMP_{r (t)$ admits a maximum $r$-multipacking of size exactly $t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: We denote $\MMP_{n-1 (t)$ as $\MMP(t)$, i.e. $\MMP(t)$ is the smallest number such that any point set $P \subset \mathbb{R ^2$ of size $ \MMP(t)$ admits a maximum multipacking of size exactly $t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: It can be followed that $\MMP(1)=1$ from the definition of multipacking.
elements.sent-2411-038: No general solution has been found for this problem yet.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our Contribution: In Section \ref{sec:Multipacking in geometry , we provide an algorithm to find a maximum multipacking of a point set on a line and we prove some bounds on the multipacking number on a line.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In Section \ref{sec:MP , we prove that $\MMP(2)=6$ using some geometric observations.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Until now, there is no known polynomial-time algorithm to find a maximum multipacking for a given point set $P\subset \mathbb{R ^2$, and the problem is also not known to be NP-hard.}
elements.sent-2411-042: In this paper we make an important step towards solving the problem by studying the $1$-multipacking and $2$-multipacking in $\mathbb{R ^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In Section \ref{sec:1,1/2 Multipacking in a 2D plane , we considered the NNG (Nearest-Neighbor-Graph) $G$ of $P\subset \mathbb{R ^2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Using the observation that the maximum independent sets of $G$ are the maximum $1$-multipackings of $P$, we conclude that a maximum $1$-multipacking can be computed in polynomial time.
elements.sent-2411-045: However the $2$-multipacking problem in $\mathbb{R ^2$ is much more complicated.}
elements.sent-2411-046: In Section \ref{sec:2,1/2 Multipacking in a 2D plane , we study the hardness of the $2$-multipacking problem in $\mathbb{R ^2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: We prove that the problem is NP-complete by reducing the well-known NP-complete problem Planar Rectilinear Monotone 3-SAT~\cite{de2012optimal to the $2$-multipacking problem.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Moreover, we provide approximation and parameterized solutions to this problem.

elements.sent-2411-071: The correctness of the algorithm is presented in the appendix [proof of Theorem \ref{thm:rk1D ].. \RestyleAlgo{ruled.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \SetAlgoLined \KwIn{A point set $P$ in $\mathbb{R}$ and $M \subseteq P$ and the value of $r$.} \KwOut{if $M$ is a valid multipacking on $P$ then return $1$,\ else return $0$} \For{$p_i$}{ \For{$s=1 \rightarrow r$}{ \eIf{$|N_s[p_i] \cap M| \leq (s+1)/2$}{continue;}{return $0$;} } } return $1$\; \caption{Check r-multipacking - $CMP_{r}(P,M)$} \label{algo:chk_2_mp}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \SetAlgoLined \KwIn{A point set $P$ in $\mathbb{R}$ and the value of $r$.} \KwOut{A maximum multipacking $M$ of $P$.} $M=\phi$\; $i=1$\; \For{$p_i$}{ $M = M \cup \{p_i\}$\; \If{$CMP_{r}(P,M) ==0$}{$M = M \setminus \{p_i\}$\;} $i++$\; } return $M$\; \caption{Maximum $r$-multipacking} \label{algo:2_mp}

elements.sent-2411-096: We write this observation as the following lemma.
elements.sent-2411-097: Let $P$ be a point set on the $2$D plane and the $NNG$ of $P$ be $G$.
elements.sent-2411-098: The maximum independent sets of $G$ are the maximum $1$-multipackings of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-099: We are interested in showing the hardness of finding a $2$-multipacking of a given set of points $P$ in the plane.
elements.sent-2411-100: We construct a graph $G_P=(V,E)$ from the given point set $P$.
elements.sent-2411-102: For every vertex $v \in V$, we introduce three edges $vu_1,vu_2,$ and $u_1u_2$ where $u_1,u_2 \in V$ are the first and second neighbors of $v$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-103: The following lemma shows the relation between the independent set of $G_P$ and a $2$-multipacking of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-104: A set $M$ is a $2$-multipacking of $P$ if and only if the vertices in the graph $G_P$ corresponding to the members of $M$ form an independent set in $G_P$.
elements.sent-2411-105: It follows from the definition of $2$-multipacking that for every tuple $(v,u_1,$ $u_2)$, as described above, at most one can be chosen as a member of $M$.
elements.sent-2411-106: Since each edge of $G_P$ is incident on a pair of vertices appearing in a tuple, there is no edge between two members of $M$ appearing in $G_P$.
elements.sent-2411-107: Thus the vertices corresponding to the members of $M$ form an independent set of $G_P$.
elements.sent-2411-108: The converse follows from a similar argument.. $\square$.
elements.sent-2411-109: Thus, finding a maximum independent set of $G_P$ is equivalent to finding a maximum $2$-multipacking of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-110: In the rest of this section, we show that finding a maximum independent set of $G_P$ is NP-complete, and we devise approximation and parameterized solutions to this problem.
elements.sent-2411-111: We show that finding the maximum independent set in the graph $G_P$ is NP-complete by reducing the well-known NP-complete problem Planar Rectilinear Monotone (PRM) 3-SAT~\cite{de2012optimal to this problem.}
elements.sent-2411-112: This variant of the boolean formula contains three literals in each clause and all of them are either positive or negative.

elements.sent-2411-114: The rectangles representing the variables lie on the $x$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-115: Whereas rectangles representing the clauses that contain the positive (negative) literals lie above (below) the $x$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-116: Additionally, the rectangles for the clauses can be connected with vertical lines to the rectangles for the variables that appear in the clauses.
elements.sent-2411-117: Given a PRM 3-SAT formula $\phi$ with $t(>1)$ variables and $m(>1)$ clauses, we construct a planar point set $P_\phi$ such that $\phi$ is satisfiable if and only if $G_{P_\phi}$ has a maximum independent set of a given size.
elements.sent-2411-118: First, we construct a point set for the variable gadget for each variable that has only two subsets as the maximum independent set in $G_{P_\phi}$.
elements.sent-2411-119: Then we construct another point set as clause gadgets for each clause in $\phi$ such that the clause gadget can incorporate only one vertex in the independent set only if at least one variable gadget has the desired set chosen as the maximum independent set.
elements.sent-2411-120: The clause and variable gadgets are connected via connection gadgets which are two point sets referred to as translation and rotation gadgets.

elements.sent-2411-277: Here we study the multipacking problems for geometric point sets with respect to their Euclidean distances.
elements.sent-2411-278: For a natural number $r \leq n-1$, an $r$-multipacking is a set $ M \subseteq P $ such that for each point $ v \in P $ and for every integer $ 1\leq s \leq r $, $|N_s[v]\cap M|\leq (s+1)/2$.
elements.sent-2411-279: For $r=1 \text{ and 2$, we study the problem of computing a maximum $r$-multipacking of the point sets in $\mathbb{R ^2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-280: We show that a maximum $1$-multipacking can be computed in polynomial time but computing a maximum $2$-multipacking is NP-hard.
elements.sent-2411-281: In this paper, we study the Multipacking \cite{teshima2012broadcasts problem in Euclidean space, which resembles the obnoxious facility location problem in computational geometry.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Given a point set $P \subseteq \mathbb{R ^2$ of size $n$, the neighborhood of size $r$ of a point $v \in P$ is the subset of $P$ that consists of the $r$ nearest points of $v$ and $v$ itself.}
elements.sent-2411-283: For a natural number $r \leq n-1$, an $r$-multipacking is a subset $ M $ of $ P $, such that for each point $ v \in P $ and for every integer $ 1\leq s \leq r $, $|N_s[v]\cap M|\leq (s+1)/2$, i.e. $M$ contains at most half of the number of elements of $N_s[v]$.
elements.sent-2411-284: An $(n-1)$-multipacking $M$ of $P$ is referred to as a multipacking of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-285: To the best of our knowledge, no significant work on multipacking has been done on planar point sets concerning their Euclidean distances.
elements.sent-2411-286: Other related works can be found in~\cite{beaudou2019multipacking,cornuejols2001combinatorial,das2023relation,das2024cactusmultipacking,meir1975relations .}
elements.sent-2411-287: A survey on the multipacking on graphs is given in the book \cite{haynes2021structures .}
elements.sent-2411-288: Our Contribution: First, we study the multipackings of the point sets on $\mathbb{R ^1$.}
elements.sent-2411-289: We start by bounding the multipacking number of $P\subset \mathbb{R ^1$ and we have shown the tightness of the bounds by constructing examples.}
elements.sent-2411-291: Further, we define the function $\MMP_{r (t)$ to be the smallest number such that any point set $P \subset \mathbb{R ^2$ of size $ \MMP_{r (t)$ admits an $r$-multipacking of size at least $t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: We denote $\MMP_{n-1 (t)$ as $\MMP(t)$, i.e. $\MMP(t)$ is the smallest number such that any point set $P \subset \mathbb{R ^2$ of size $ \MMP(t)$ admits a multipacking of size at least $t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-293: We provide the value of $\MMP(2)$ in the following theorem: \begin{restatable {theorem {MPtwo $\MMP(2)=6$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-296: We have studied the hardness of the $2$-multipacking problem in $\mathbb{R ^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-297: We reduce the well-known NP-complete problem Planar Rectilinear Monotone 3-SAT~\cite{de2012optimal to our problem to show the following.}
elements.sent-2411-298: We have shown that a maximum $2$-multipacking of a given set of points $P$ in $\mathbb{R ^2$ can be approximated in polynomial time within a ratio arbitrarily close to $4$ and $2$-multipacking of size $k$ of a given set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R ^2$ can be computed in time $18^kn^{O(1)}$, if it exists.}}

elements.sent-2411-314: In the rest of this section, we show that finding a maximum independent set of $G_P$ is NP-hard, and we devise approximation and parameterized solutions to this problem.

elements.sent-2411-324: This paper studies some multipacking problems in a geometric setting under the Euclidean distance metric.
elements.sent-2411-325: An intriguing open topic in this area is addressing the difficulty of computing a maximum multipacking ($(n-1)$-multipacking) in general.
elements.sent-2411-326: In this paper, we have given the value of $\MMP(2)$.
elements.sent-2411-327: A natural direction for future research is determining the exact values of $\MMP(t)$ for $t \geq 3$.
elements.sent-2411-328: Additionally, the definition of $r$-multipacking in geometry, inspired by obnoxious facility location and graph-theoretic multipacking, can be generalized by introducing a fractional parameter $k \in [0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-329: Specifically, the condition on a set $M$ could be generalized to $|N_s[v] \cap M| \leq k(s+1)$ for each $v \in P$.
elements.sent-2411-330: Varying $k$ may lead to different bounds on the multipacking number and introduce new computational challenges.
elements.sent-2411-331: Further extensions of this work could explore multipackings in higher-dimensional Euclidean spaces $\mathbb{R ^d$, where geometric constraints and packing densities may yield richer structural and algorithmic results.. \bibliographystyle{elsarticle-num.}}
